title: Farinata
subtitle: Ligurian chickpea flour pancake baked in a copper pan
excerpt: Farinata is Genoa's street food: a thin, crisp-edged chickpea flour pancake baked at high heat in a film of olive oil. The batter is nothing but chickpea flour, water, olive oil, and salt, left to rest for several hours so the flour fully hydrates. It bakes in 10 minutes and comes out of the oven with a golden, slightly blistered surface and a creamy, custard-like interior.
sourceNotes: Farinata (also called cecina in Tuscany and socca in Nice) has deep Ligurian roots. Pellegrino Artusi, 'La Scienza in Cucina' (1891), Project Gutenberg, includes a farinata entry describing the simple batter and high-heat method. The dish is documented in fourteenth-century Genoese records as a cheap street food. The long batter rest is critical and appears in all historic accounts; the flour needs time to fully hydrate.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter can rest in the fridge for up to 12 hours. Bring to room temperature and skim off any foam before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven at maximum heat; grill element on for the final 2 minutes if available

glossaryTerms.definition: Chickpea flour absorbs water more slowly than wheat flour. The long rest — at least 4 hours — is not optional; it allows the starch granules to fully hydrate and the batter to thicken evenly. A batter poured too soon will be thin in places and floury in others. The surface foam that forms during the rest contains bitterness from the chickpeas; skim it off before baking.

content.text: The long rest is the recipe. Allow at least 4 hours (overnight is better) for the
. Skim the foam off the surface before baking; it tastes bitter. The olive oil goes into the batter, not just the pan; this is what gives the farinata its characteristic custardy interior.
Mix and rest, at least 4 hours

Whisk 200 g chickpea flour into 600 ml cold water, adding the water gradually to avoid lumps. Whisk in 60 ml olive oil and 8 g salt until smooth. Cover and leave at room temperature for at least 4 hours, or overnight in the fridge. A foam will form on the surface; skim this off with a spoon just before baking.

Heat the oven to its maximum temperature; at least 240 degrees C fan. Place a 28 to 30 cm cast-iron pan or heavy ovenproof frying pan inside and heat for 10 minutes until smoking hot. Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more; use an oven thermometer if you have one.

Carefully remove the hot pan. Pour in 2 tbsp olive oil and swirl to coat. Immediately pour in the batter; it should sizzle. Scatter rosemary leaves over the surface if using. Bake for 8 to 10 minutes until the edges are dark gold and crisp and the centre is set but still slightly trembling. Switch to the grill for 2 minutes to blister the surface. Grind black pepper over it at the table. Cut into wedges and serve immediately; farinata does not keep.

items.problem: Farinata is floury and grainy in the centre
items.solution: The batter did not rest for long enough. The chickpea flour needs a minimum of 4 hours to hydrate; less and the starch granules remain intact and taste raw. Rest overnight if possible.

items.problem: Stuck to the pan and tore when cut
items.solution: The pan was not hot enough when the batter went in, or not enough oil was used. The pan must be smoking before the batter is poured, and the oil must coat the entire base.
The foam on the batter was not skimmed before baking. The foam concentrates saponins from the chickpeas, which taste bitter when baked. Skim it carefully with a spoon before pouring.
